votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by mathematically computing the exact number of valid triples using combinations, which matches the length of the list of valid triples defined in Coq.

The Python code correctly implements the combinatorial logic derived from the modular properties of the 'elem' function specified in Coq. It accurately counts the number of triples (i, j, k) satisfying the condition by separating indices based on their value modulo 3.
The Python code correctly implements the mathematical logic to count the number of valid triples by analyzing the values modulo 3, which exactly matches the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the spec. elem(i) = i*i - i + 1. For i%3==0: elem=1, i%3==1: elem=1, i%3==2: elem=3≡0(mod 3). So elem(i)%3 is 0 when i%3==2, else 1. For sum%3==0 with values in {0,1}, need sum=0 (all zeros) or sum=3 (all ones). The counting of indices congruent to 2 mod 3 in [1,n] as n//3 + (1 if n%3==2 else 0) is correct, and nC3 correctly counts combinations. The spec asks if result equals the count of valid triples, which the Python code computes correctly.

The Python code correctly implements the spec. elem(i) = i*i - i + 1. For i%3==0: elem=1, i%3==1: elem=1, i%3==2: elem=3≡0(mod 3). So elem(i)%3 is 0 when i%3==2, else 1. For sum%3==0 with values in {0,1}, need sum=0 (all zeros) or sum=3 (all ones). The counting of indices congruent to 2 mod 3 in [1,n] is correct: n//3 + (1 if n%3==2 else 0). The combination formula nC3 correctly counts triples from each group. The Python spec returns true iff result equals this count, matching the Coq spec which states result equals the length of the list of all valid triples.

The Python code correctly implements the spec. It computes elem(i) = i*i - i + 1, and valid_triple checks the same conditions as Coq. The spec function correctly counts valid triples: elem(x) % 3 is 0 when x % 3 == 2, and 1 otherwise. For sum % 3 == 0, we need either all three elem values to be 0 (all indices ≡ 2 mod 3) or all three to be 1 (no index ≡ 2 mod 3). The counting of indices congruent to 2 mod 3 in range [1,n] is correct: count_zeros = n//3 + (1 if n%3==2 else 0), which equals the count of {2, 5, 8, ...} up to n. The combination formula nC3 is correct. The result matches the Coq spec which defines result as the count of valid triples.

The Python computes the count of triples 1<=i<j<k<=n with (elem(i)+elem(j)+elem(k))%3==0 by using that elem(i)%3 is 0 iff i%3==2 and 1 otherwise, so valid triples are either all from class {i%3==2} or all from its complement. It returns C(c0,3)+C(c1,3) with correct edge handling for n<3, matching the Coq spec’s count.

Counts triples via residue classes matching elem(i) mod 3 behavior; only all-zeros (i≡2 mod 3) or all-ones (i≡0,1 mod 3) contribute, yielding C(count_zeros,3)+C(count_ones,3). Handles n<3 as 0. This matches the Coq spec’s count of valid triples.

It counts the number of valid triples by analyzing elem(i) mod 3: elem(i)%3 is 0 iff i%3==2, else 1. Triples sum to 0 mod 3 only when all three are from the same class, so the count is C(count_zeros,3)+C(count_ones,3). This matches the Coq spec for all n (including n<3 yielding 0).
